title: Non-governmental organization

infobox.value: (**NGO**) is an entity that is not part of the [government](/wiki/Government "Government"). This can include non-profit and for-profit entities. An NGO may get a significant percentage or even all of its funding from government sources. An NGO typically is thought to be a [nonprofit organization](/wiki/Nonprofit_organization "Nonprofit organization") that operates partially independent of government control. Non-profit NGOs often focus on humanitarian or social issues but can also include clubs and associations offering services to members. Some non-profit NGOs, like the [World Economic Forum](/wiki/World_Economic_Forum "World Economic Forum"), may also act as lobby groups for corporations. Unlike [intergovernmental organizations](/wiki/Intergovernmental_organizations "Intergovernmental organizations") (IGOs), which directly interact with sovereign states and governments, NGOs are independent from them.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Entity that is not part of the government

"NGO" redirects here. For other uses, see [NGO (disambiguation)](/wiki/NGO_(disambiguation) "NGO (disambiguation)").

A  **non-governmental organization** (**NGO**) is an entity that is not part of the [government](/wiki/Government "Government"). This can include non-profit and for-profit entities. An NGO may get a significant percentage or even all of its funding from government sources. An NGO typically is thought to be a [nonprofit organization](/wiki/Nonprofit_organization "Nonprofit organization") that operates partially independent of government control. Non-profit NGOs often focus on humanitarian or social issues but can also include clubs and associations offering services to members. Some non-profit NGOs, like the [World Economic Forum](/wiki/World_Economic_Forum "World Economic Forum"), may also act as lobby groups for corporations. Unlike [intergovernmental organizations](/wiki/Intergovernmental_organizations "Intergovernmental organizations") (IGOs), which directly interact with sovereign states and governments, NGOs are independent from them.

The term as it is used today was first introduced in [Article 71](/wiki/Article_71_of_the_UN_Charter "Article 71 of the UN Charter") of the newly formed [United Nations Charter](/wiki/United_Nations_Charter "United Nations Charter") in 1945.[[1]](#cite_note-:4-1) While there is no fixed or formal definition for what NGOs are, they are generally defined as non-profit entities that are independent of government management or direction—although they may receive [government funding](/wiki/Government_funding "Government funding").[[1]](#cite_note-:4-1)

According to the [UN Department of Global Communications](/wiki/United_Nations_Department_of_Global_Communications "United Nations Department of Global Communications"), an NGO is "a [not-for profit](/wiki/Not-for-Profit "Not-for-Profit"), [voluntary](/wiki/Voluntary_sector "Voluntary sector") citizen's group that is organized on a local, national or international level to address issues in support of the public good".[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) The term *NGO* is used inconsistently, and is sometimes used synonymously with *[civil society](/wiki/Civil_society "Civil society") organization* (CSO), which is any association founded by citizens.[[3]](#cite_note-usstate-3) In some countries, NGOs are known as non-profit organizations while [political parties](/wiki/Political_party "Political party") and [trade unions](/wiki/Trade_union "Trade union") are sometimes considered NGOs as well.[[4]](#cite_note-:9-4)

NGOs are classified by (1) orientation- entailing the type of activities an NGO undertakes, such as activities involving [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights "Human rights"), [consumer protection](/wiki/Consumer_protection "Consumer protection"), [environmentalism](/wiki/Environmentalism "Environmentalism"), [health](/wiki/Health "Health"), or development; and (2) level of operation, which indicates the scale at which an organization works: local, regional, national, or international.[[4]](#cite_note-:9-4)

The number of NGOs varies widely by country. India has one of the largest sectors, with over 3.7 million NGOs registered on the government's DARPAN portal as of 2024.[[5]](#cite_note-auto-5) In contrast, the number of registered NGOs in Russia has seen a significant decline due to restrictive laws, with thousands being liquidated or delisted since the implementation of the "foreign agent" law.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The United States, by comparison, has approximately 1.5 million NGOs; an NGO for every 227 people.[[7]](#cite_note-state.gov-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Types

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-governmental_organization&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Types")]

NGOs further the social goals of their members (or founders): improving the [natural environment](/wiki/Natural_environment "Natural environment"), encouraging the observance of [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights "Human rights"), improving the welfare of the disadvantaged, or representing a corporate agenda. Their goals cover a wide range of issues. They may fund local NGOs, institutions and projects, and implement projects.[[9]](#cite_note-Lawry_2009_29-30-9)

NGOs can be in the following ways:[[4]](#cite_note-:9-4)

1. orientation, i.e. the type of activities an NGO undertakes, such as activities involving [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights "Human rights"), [consumer protection](/wiki/Consumer_protection "Consumer protection"), [environmentalism](/wiki/Environmentalism "Environmentalism"), [health](/wiki/Health "Health"), or development.
2. level of operation, which indicates the scale at which an organization works: local, regional, national, or international.

### Orientation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-governmental_organization&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Orientation")]

* [Charity](/wiki/Charitable_organization "Charitable organization") — often a [top-down](/wiki/Top-down_and_bottom-up_design "Top-down and bottom-up design") effort, with little participation or input from [beneficiaries](/wiki/Beneficiary "Beneficiary"). They include NGOs directed at meeting the needs of disadvantaged people and groups.
* [Service](/wiki/Service_organisation "Service organisation") — includes NGOs that provide [healthcare](/wiki/Health_care "Health care") (including [family planning](/wiki/Family_planning "Family planning")) and [education](/wiki/Education "Education").
* [Participatory](/wiki/Participatory_organization "Participatory organization") — [self-help projects](/wiki/Self-help_groups "Self-help groups") with local involvement in the form of [money](/wiki/Self-help_group_(finance) "Self-help group (finance)"), tools, land, materials, or labor
* Empowerment — aim to help poor people to understand the social, political, and economic factors affecting their lives, and to increase awareness of their power to control their lives. With maximum involvement by the beneficiaries, the NGOs are facilitators.[[9]](#cite_note-Lawry_2009_29-30-9)
* [Professional](/wiki/Professional_association "Professional association") [(trade) union](/wiki/Trade_union "Trade union") [(association)](/wiki/Trade_association "Trade association") – a voluntary association or union of people with common interests including sports, culture, others, i.e. [Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences](/wiki/Academy_of_Motion_Picture_Arts_and_Sciences "Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences"), [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA")

### Level of operation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-governmental_organization&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Level of operation")]

* [Community-based organizations](/wiki/Community-based_organizations "Community-based organizations") (CBOs) — popular initiatives which can [raise the consciousness](/wiki/Consciousness_raising "Consciousness raising") of the [urban poor](/wiki/Urban_poverty "Urban poverty"), helping them understand their right to services, and providing such services.
* City-wide organizations — include [chambers of commerce and industry](/wiki/Chamber_of_commerce_and_industry "Chamber of commerce and industry"), [coalitions](/wiki/Coalition "Coalition") of business, ethnic or [educational](/wiki/Educational_institution "Educational institution") groups, and [community organizations](/wiki/Community_organizing "Community organizing").
* State NGOs — include state-level organizations, associations, and groups. Some state NGOs are guided by national and international NGOs.
* National NGOs — an NGO that exists in only one country.[[10]](#cite_note-WillettsCS-NTWKS-10) These include national organizations such as [YMCAs](/wiki/YMCA "YMCA") and [YWCAs](/wiki/YWCA "YWCA"), [professional associations](/wiki/Professional_association "Professional association"), and similar groups. Some have state or city branches, and assist local NGOs.
* [International NGOs](/wiki/International_non-governmental_organization "International non-governmental organization") (INGOs) — range from secular agencies, such as [Save the Children](/wiki/Save_the_Children "Save the Children"), to religious groups. They may fund local NGOs, institutions and projects, and implement projects.[[9]](#cite_note-Lawry_2009_29-30-9)

### Other terms/acronyms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-governmental_organization&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Other terms/acronyms")]

Similar terms include [third-sector](/wiki/Voluntary_sector "Voluntary sector") organization (TSO), [non-profit organization](/wiki/Non-profit_organization "Non-profit organization") (NPO), voluntary organization (VO), [civil society](/wiki/Civil_society "Civil society") organization (CSO), [grassroots](/wiki/Grassroots "Grassroots") organization (GO), [social movement organization](/wiki/Social_movement_organization "Social movement organization") (SMO), private voluntary organization (PVO), [self-help](/wiki/Self-help "Self-help") organization (SHO), and [non-state actors](/wiki/Non-state_actor "Non-state actor") (NSAs). Numerous variations exist for the NGO acronym, either due to language, region, or specificity.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Some [Romance languages](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages") use the synonymous abbreviation *ONG*; for example:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *organisation non gouvernementale*
* [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *organizzazione non governativa*
* [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *organização não governmental*
* [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *organización no gubernamental*
* [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language"): *organizație neguvernamentală*

Other acronyms that are typically used to describe non-governmental organizations include:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* BINGO: Business-friendly international NGO or Big international NGO
* CSO: [Civil society](/wiki/Civil_society "Civil society") organization
* [ENGO](/wiki/ENGO "ENGO"): Environmental NGO — organizations that advocate for the environment, such as [Greenpeace](/wiki/Greenpeace "Greenpeace") and the [WWF](/wiki/World_Wide_Fund_for_Nature "World Wide Fund for Nature").[[11]](#cite_note-:10-11)
* DONGO: Donor-organized NGO
* GONGO: [Government-organized non-governmental organization](/wiki/Government-organized_non-governmental_organization "Government-organized non-governmental organization") — often used derogatorily, these are government-backed NGOs that are set up to advocate on behalf of a repressive regime on the international stage.[[11]](#cite_note-:10-11)
* GSO: [Grassroots Support Organization](/wiki/Grassroots_Support_Organization "Grassroots Support Organization")
* INGO: [International NGO](/wiki/International_non-governmental_organization "International non-governmental organization")[[11]](#cite_note-:10-11)
* MANGO: Market advocacy NGO
* NGDO: Non-governmental development organization
* NNGO: Northern (UK) NGO
* PANGO: Party NGO — addressing political matters
* PVDO: Private voluntary development organization[[12]](#cite_note-12) — the [United States Agency for International Development](/wiki/United_States_Agency_for_International_Development "United States Agency for International Development") (USAID) refers to NGOs as "private voluntary organizations".[[13]](#cite_note-13)
* [Quango](/wiki/Quango "Quango"): Quasi-autonomous NGO — often used derogatorily, these organizations rely on [public funding](/wiki/Public_funding "Public funding").[[11]](#cite_note-:10-11) They are prevalent in the United Kingdom (where there are more than 1,200), Ireland, and the [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations").
* SBO: [Social benefit organization](/wiki/Social_benefit_organization "Social benefit organization") — a goal-oriented designation
* SCO: [Social change](/wiki/Social_change "Social change") organization
* SNGO: Southern (UK) NGO
* TANGO: Technical assistance NGO
* TNGO: [Transnational NGO](/wiki/Transnational_organization "Transnational organization") — coined during the 1970s due to the increase of environmental and economic issues in the global community. TNGOs exist in two (or more) countries.
* YOUNGO: Youth NGOs - advocacing for youth rights.

## Activities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-governmental_organization&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Activities")]

[![A roomful of people](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Europe_in_a_suitcase_-_UK.jpg/250px-Europe_in_a_suitcase_-_UK.jpg)](/wiki/File:Europe_in_a_suitcase_-_UK.jpg)

[Europe-Georgia Institute](/wiki/Europe-Georgia_Institute "Europe-Georgia Institute") head [George Melashvili](/wiki/George_Melashvili "George Melashvili") addresses the audience at the launch of the "Europe in a suitcase" project by two NGOs (the EGI and the [Friedrich Naumann Foundation](/wiki/Friedrich_Naumann_Foundation "Friedrich Naumann Foundation")), which aims to increase cooperation between European politicians, journalists and representatives of the civic sector and academia with their counterparts in [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(country) "Georgia (country)").[[14]](#cite_note-egisutcase2-14)

Non-governmental organizations (NGOs) play a vital role in improving the lives of people who have been affected by natural disasters or are facing other challenges. NGOs can act as implementers, catalysts, and partners to provide essential goods and services to those in need. They work to mobilize resources, both financial and human, to ensure that aid is delivered in a timely and effective manner.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

NGOs also play a critical role in driving change by advocating for policies and practices that benefit disadvantaged communities. They often work in partnership with other organizations, including government agencies, to address complex challenges that require a collaborative approach.[[15]](#cite_note-15) One of the key strengths of NGOs is their ability to work at the grassroots level and to connect with communities directly. This allows them to gain a deep understanding of the issues facing people and to tailor their services to meet the specific needs of each community.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

NGOs vary by method; some are primarily [advocacy groups](/wiki/Advocacy_group "Advocacy group"), and others conduct programs and activities. [Oxfam](/wiki/Oxfam "Oxfam"), concerned with poverty alleviation, may provide needy people with the equipment and skills to obtain food and [drinking water](/wiki/Drinking_water "Drinking water"); the [Forum for Fact-finding Documentation and Advocacy](/wiki/Forum_for_Fact-finding_Documentation_and_Advocacy "Forum for Fact-finding Documentation and Advocacy") (FFDA) helps provide legal assistance to victims of human-rights abuses. The [Afghanistan Information Management Services](/wiki/Afghanistan_Information_Management_Services "Afghanistan Information Management Services") provide specialized technical products and services to support development activities implemented on the ground by other organizations. Management techniques are crucial to project success.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

The [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") classifies NGO activity into two general categories:[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[17]](#cite_note-definition-17)[[11]](#cite_note-:10-11)

1. operational NGOs, whose primary function is the design and implementation of development-related projects
2. advocacy NGOs, whose primary function is to defend or promote a particular cause and who seek to influence the policies and practices of [International governmental organisations](/wiki/International_governmental_organisation "International governmental organisation") (IGOs).

NGOs may also conduct both activities: operational NGOs will use campaigning techniques if they face issues in the field, which could be remedied by policy change, and campaigning NGOs (such as [human-rights organizations](/wiki/Human_rights_organizations "Human rights organizations")) often have programs which assist individual victims for whom they are trying to advocate.[[10]](#cite_note-WillettsCS-NTWKS-10)[[11]](#cite_note-:10-11)

### Operational

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-governmental_organization&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Operational")]

**Operational NGOs** seek to "achieve small-scale change directly through projects",[[10]](#cite_note-WillettsCS-NTWKS-10) mobilizing financial resources, materials, and volunteers to create local programs. They hold large-scale [fundraising](/wiki/Fundraising "Fundraising") events and may apply to governments and organizations for grants or contracts to raise money for projects. Operational NGOs often have a [hierarchical](/wiki/Hierarchy "Hierarchy") structure; their headquarters are staffed by professionals who plan projects, create budgets, keep accounts, and report to and communicate with operational fieldworkers on projects.[[10]](#cite_note-WillettsCS-NTWKS-10) They are most often associated with the delivery of services or environmental issues, emergency relief, and public welfare. Operational NGOs may be subdivided into relief or development organizations, service-delivery or participatory, religious or secular, and public or private. Although operational NGOs may be community-based, many are national or international. The defining activity of an operational NGO is the implementation of projects.[[10]](#cite_note-WillettsCS-NTWKS-10)

### Advocacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-governmental_organization&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Advocacy")]

**Advocacy NGOs** or campaigning NGOs seek to "achieve large-scale change promoted indirectly through the influence of the political system".[[10]](#cite_note-WillettsCS-NTWKS-10) They require an active, efficient group of professional members who can keep supporters informed and motivated. Campaigning NGOs must plan and host demonstrations and events which will attract media, their defining activity.[[10]](#cite_note-WillettsCS-NTWKS-10)

Campaigning NGOs often deal with issues related to human rights, women's rights, and children's rights, and their primary purpose is to defend (or promote) a specific cause.[[10]](#cite_note-WillettsCS-NTWKS-10)

### Public relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-governmental_organization&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Public relations")]

Non-governmental organisations need healthy [public relations](/wiki/Public_relations "Public relations") in order to meet their goals, and use sophisticated public-relations campaigns to raise funds and deal with governments. Interest groups may be politically important, influencing social and political outcomes. A [code of ethics](/wiki/Ethical_code "Ethical code") was established in 2002 by the [World Association of Non-Governmental Organizations](/wiki/Unification_Church#Family_Federation_for_World_Peace_and_Unification_(1994–present) "Unification Church").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## Structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-governmental_organization&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Structure")]

### Staffing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-governmental_organization&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Staffing")]

Some NGOs rely on paid staff while others are based on [volunteers](/wiki/Volunteering "Volunteering"). Although many NGOs use international staff in developing countries, others rely on local employees or volunteers. Foreign staff may satisfy a [donor](/wiki/Donation "Donation") who wants to see the supported project managed by a person from an [industrialized country](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country"). The expertise of these employees (or volunteers) may be counterbalanced by several factors, such as; the cost of [foreigners](/wiki/Alien_(law) "Alien (law)") is typically higher, they have no [grassroots connections](/wiki/Grassroots_democracy "Grassroots democracy") in the country, and local expertise may be undervalued.[[17]](#cite_note-definition-17) By the end of 1995, [Concern Worldwide](/wiki/Concern_Worldwide "Concern Worldwide") (an international anti-poverty NGO) employed 174 foreigners and just over 5,000 local staff in [Haiti](/wiki/Haiti "Haiti") and ten developing countries in Africa and Asia.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

On average, employees in NGOs earn 11-12% less compared to employees of for-profit organizations and government workers with the same number of qualifications .[[19]](#cite_note-19) However, in many cases NGOs employees receive more fringe benefits.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### Funding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-governmental_organization&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Funding")]

NGOs are usually funded by donations, but some avoid formal funding and are run by volunteers. NGOs may have [charitable status](/wiki/Charitable_organization "Charitable organization"), or may be [tax-exempt](/wiki/Tax-exempt "Tax-exempt") in recognition of their social, political, religious, or other purposes. Since the end of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), NGOs have had an increased role in [international development](/wiki/International_development "International development"),[[21]](#cite_note-21) particularly in the fields of humanitarian assistance and poverty alleviation.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Funding sources include membership dues, the sale of [goods](/wiki/Product_(business) "Product (business)") and services, grants from international institutions or national governments, [corporate social responsibility](/wiki/Corporate_social_responsibility "Corporate social responsibility") (CSR) funds and private donations. Although the term "non-governmental organization" implies [independence](/wiki/Independence "Independence") from governments, many NGOs depend on government funding;[[23]](#cite_note-global-23) one-fourth of [Oxfam](/wiki/Oxfam "Oxfam")'s US$162 million 1998 income was donated by the British government and the EU, and [World Vision United States](/wiki/World_Vision_United_States "World Vision United States") collected $55 million worth of goods in 1998 from the American government. Several EU grants provide funds accessible to NGOs.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Government funding of NGOs is controversial, since "the whole point of humanitarian intervention was precise that NGOs and civil society had both a right and an obligation to respond with acts of aid and solidarity to people in need or being subjected to repression or want by the forces that controlled them, whatever the governments concerned might think about the matter."[[24]](#cite_note-24) Some NGOs, such as [Greenpeace](/wiki/Greenpeace "Greenpeace"), do not accept funding from governments or intergovernmental organizations.[[25]](#cite_note-HBS-25)[[26]](#cite_note-GP_Annual_report_2008-26) The 1999 budget of the American Association of Retired Persons ([AARP](/wiki/AARP "AARP")) was over $540 million.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

In America, government funding of NGOs relating to immigration is common, and is one of the stated methods the Office of Refugee Resettlement uses to help integrate immigrants to America.[[28]](#cite_note-28) Government funding sometimes accounts for the vast majority of overall funding for these NGOs, for example Global Refuge received 180 million dollars of its 207 million dollar budget from federal funding.[[29]](#cite_note-29) In recent years, government contracts to non-profits have exploded both in number and size. The Budget for the Office of Refugee Resettlement has increased from 1.8 billion in 2018 to 6.3 billion in 2022.[[30]](#cite_note-30) Critics point to the million-dollar salaries of CEOS and the use of funds for "music therapy" and "pet therapy" as a worrying sign that the money might not be appropriated to help the migrant crisis, but rather as a political move to keep wealthy backers loyal.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Overhead

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-governmental_organization&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Overhead")]

[Overhead](/wiki/Overhead_(business) "Overhead (business)") is the amount of money spent on running an NGO, rather than on projects.[[31]](#cite_note-Overhead1-31) It includes office expenses,[[31]](#cite_note-Overhead1-31) salaries, and banking and bookkeeping costs. An NGO's percentage of its overall budget spent on overhead is often used to judge it; less than four percent is considered good.[[31]](#cite_note-Overhead1-31) According to the World Association of Non-Governmental Organizations, more than 86 percent should be spent on programs (less than 20 percent on overhead).[[32]](#cite_note-32) [The Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria](/wiki/The_Global_Fund_to_Fight_AIDS,_Tuberculosis_and_Malaria "The Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria") has guidelines of five to seven percent overhead to receive funding;[[33]](#cite_note-33) the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") typically allows 37 percent.[[34]](#cite_note-34) A high percentage of overhead relative to total expenditures can make it more difficult to generate funds.[[35]](#cite_note-35) High overhead costs may also generate public criticism.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

A sole focus on overhead, however, can be counterproductive.[[37]](#cite_note-37) Research published by the [Urban Institute](/wiki/Urban_Institute "Urban Institute") and [Stanford University](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University")'s Center for Social Innovation have shown that rating agencies create incentives for NGOs to lower (and hide) overhead costs, which may reduce organizational effectiveness by starving organizations of infrastructure to deliver services.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-ssireview-39) An alternative rating system would provide, in addition to financial data, a qualitative evaluation of an organization's transparency and governance:

1. An assessment of program effectiveness
2. Evaluation of feedback mechanisms for donors and beneficiaries
3. Allowing a rated organization to respond to an evaluation by a rating agency[[39]](#cite_note-ssireview-39)

### Monitoring and control

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-governmental_organization&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Monitoring and control")]

In a March 2000 report on United Nations reform priorities, former UN Secretary-General [Kofi Annan](/wiki/Kofi_Annan "Kofi Annan") favored international humanitarian intervention as the [responsibility to protect](/wiki/Responsibility_to_protect "Responsibility to protect")[[40]](#cite_note-40) citizens from ethnic cleansing, genocide, and crimes against humanity. After that report, the Canadian government launched its Responsibility to Protect (R2P)[[41]](#cite_note-41) project outlining the issue of humanitarian intervention. The R2P project has wide applications, and among its more controversial has been the Canadian government's use of R2P to justify its intervention in the coup in Haiti.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

Large corporations have increased their [corporate social responsibility](/wiki/Corporate_social_responsibility "Corporate social responsibility") departments to preempt NGO campaigns against corporate practices. Collaboration between corporations and NGOs risks [co-option](/wiki/Co-option "Co-option") of the weaker partner, typically the NGO.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

In December 2007, [Assistant Secretary of Defense for Health Affairs](/wiki/Assistant_Secretary_of_Defense_for_Health_Affairs "Assistant Secretary of Defense for Health Affairs") [S. Ward Casscells](/wiki/S._Ward_Casscells "S. Ward Casscells") established an International Health Division of Force Health Protection & Readiness.[[44]](#cite_note-44) Part of International Health's mission is to communicate with NGOs about areas of mutual interest. Department of Defense Directive 3000.05,[[45]](#cite_note-45) in 2005, required the US Defense Department to regard stability-enhancing activities as equally important as combat. In compliance with [international law](/wiki/Geneva_Conventions "Geneva Conventions"), the department has developed a capacity to improve essential services in areas of conflict (such as [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq")) where customary lead agencies like the [State Department](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_State "United States Department of State") and USAID have difficulty operating. International Health cultivates collaborative, arm's-length relationships with NGOs, recognizing their independence, expertise, and honest-broker status.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Regulation and environment by country

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-governmental_organization&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Regulation and environment by country")]

### India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-governmental_organization&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: India")]

India has one of the world's largest NGO sectors, with over 3.7 million non-governmental organizations registered on the government's DARPAN portal as of 2024.[[5]](#cite_note-auto-5) These organizations are primarily registered as Trusts, Societies, or Section 8 Companies and must obtain special certifications (12A and 80G) from the Income Tax Department to be tax-exempt.

A defining feature of the Indian NGO landscape is the stringent regulation of foreign funding, governed by the Foreign Contribution (Regulation) Act, 2010 (FCRA). An FCRA registration is mandatory for any NGO wishing to receive foreign donations. The Indian government has used the act to significantly tighten control over the sector, leading to the cancellation or non-renewal of FCRA licenses for thousands of organizations.[[46]](#cite_note-46) This crackdown has particularly affected NGOs working on human rights, environmental advocacy, and civil liberties, prompting criticism from civil society groups that the law is used to suppress dissent. The government maintains that such measures are necessary to ensure accountability and prevent foreign interference in domestic affairs.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-governmental_organization&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: History")]

[International non-governmental organizations](/wiki/International_non-governmental_organization "International non-governmental organization") date back to at least the late 18th century,[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49) and there were an estimated 1,083 NGOs by 1914.[[50]](#cite_note-50) International NGOs were important to the [anti-slavery](/wiki/Abolitionism "Abolitionism") and [women's suffrage](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage "Women's suffrage") movements, and peaked at the time of the 1932–1934 [World Disarmament Conference](/wiki/World_Disarmament_Conference "World Disarmament Conference").[[51]](#cite_note-51)

The term became popular with the 1945 founding of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") in 1945;[[52]](#cite_note-52) Article 71 in [Chapter X of its charter](/wiki/Chapter_X_of_the_United_Nations_Charter "Chapter X of the United Nations Charter")[[53]](#cite_note-53) stipulated [consultative status](/wiki/Consultative_status "Consultative status") for organizations which are neither governments nor member states.[[54]](#cite_note-54) An international NGO was first defined in resolution 288 (X) of the [United Nations Economic and Social Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Economic_and_Social_Council "United Nations Economic and Social Council") (ECOSOC) on February 27, 1950, as "any international organization that is not founded by an international treaty". The role of NGOs and other "major groups" in [sustainable development](/wiki/Sustainable_development "Sustainable development") was recognized in Chapter 27[[55]](#cite_note-55) of [Agenda 21](/wiki/Agenda_21 "Agenda 21").[[56]](#cite_note-56) The rise and fall of international NGOs matches contemporary events, waxing in periods of growth and waning in times of crisis.[[57]](#cite_note-57) The United Nations gave non-governmental organizations observer status at its assemblies and some meetings. According to the UN, an NGO is a private, not-for-profit organization which is independent of government control and is not merely an opposition [political party](/wiki/Political_party "Political party").[[58]](#cite_note-58) An observer has access to most meetings and relevant documentation.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

The rapid development of the non-governmental sector occurred in Western countries as a result of the restructuring of the [welfare state](/wiki/Welfare_state "Welfare state"). [Globalization](/wiki/Globalization "Globalization") of that process occurred after the fall of the communist system, and was an important part of the [Washington Consensus](/wiki/Washington_Consensus "Washington Consensus").[[23]](#cite_note-global-23)

Twentieth-century [globalization](/wiki/Globalization "Globalization") increased the importance of NGOs. [International treaties](/wiki/Treaty "Treaty") and organizations, such as the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization"), focused on [capitalist](/wiki/Capitalism "Capitalism") interests. To counterbalance this trend, NGOs emphasize [humanitarian issues](/wiki/Humanitarianism "Humanitarianism"), [development aid](/wiki/Development_aid "Development aid"), and [sustainable development](/wiki/Sustainable_development "Sustainable development"). An example is the [World Social Forum](/wiki/World_Social_Forum "World Social Forum"), a rival convention of the [World Economic Forum](/wiki/World_Economic_Forum "World Economic Forum") held each January in [Davos](/wiki/Davos "Davos"), Switzerland. The fifth World Social Forum, in [Porto Alegre](/wiki/Porto_Alegre "Porto Alegre"), Brazil in January 2005, was attended by representatives of over 1,000 NGOs.[[60]](#cite_note-60) The 1992 [Earth Summit](/wiki/Earth_Summit "Earth Summit") in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), attended by about 2,400 representatives, was the first to demonstrate the power of international NGOs in environmental issues and sustainable development. Transnational NGO networking has become extensive.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

## Legal status

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-governmental_organization&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Legal status")]

Although NGOs are subject to national laws and practices, four main groups may be found worldwide:[[62]](#cite_note-62)

* [Unincorporated](/wiki/Unincorporated_association "Unincorporated association") and [voluntary association](/wiki/Voluntary_association "Voluntary association")
* [Trusts](/wiki/Trusts "Trusts"), [charities](/wiki/Charities "Charities"), and [foundations](/wiki/Foundation_(nonprofit) "Foundation (nonprofit)")
* [Not-for-profit companies](/wiki/Not-for-profit_companies "Not-for-profit companies") and [co-operatives](/wiki/Co-operatives "Co-operatives")
* Entities formed (or registered) under special NGO or [nonprofit](/wiki/Nonprofit_organization "Nonprofit organization") laws

The [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe") drafted the [European Convention on the Recognition of the Legal Personality of International Non-Governmental Organisations](/wiki/European_Convention_on_the_Recognition_of_the_Legal_Personality_of_International_Non-Governmental_Organisations "European Convention on the Recognition of the Legal Personality of International Non-Governmental Organisations") in [Strasbourg](/wiki/Strasbourg "Strasbourg") in 1986, creating a common legal basis for European NGOs. Article 11 of the [European Convention on Human Rights](/wiki/European_Convention_on_Human_Rights "European Convention on Human Rights") protects the right to associate, which is fundamental for NGOs.

[Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary") is the only country in the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") to severely restrict NGOs such as corruption, LGBTQ rights and migration.

## Economic theory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-governmental_organization&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Economic theory")]

The question whether a public project should be owned by an NGO or by the government has been studied in economics using the tools of the [incomplete contracting](/wiki/Incomplete_contracts "Incomplete contracts") theory.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] According to this theory, not every detail of a relationship between decision makers can be contractually specified. Hence, in the future, the parties will bargain with each other to adapt their relationship to changing circumstances. Ownership matters because it determines the parties' willingness to make non-contractible investments. In the context of private firms, [Oliver Hart](/wiki/Oliver_Hart_(economist) "Oliver Hart (economist)") has shown that the party with the more important investment task should be owner.[[63]](#cite_note-63) Yet, Besley and Ghatak have argued that in the context of public projects the investment technology does not matter.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

Specifically, even when the government is the key investor, ownership by an NGO is optimal if and only if the NGO has a larger valuation of the project than the government. However, the general validity of this argument has been questioned by follow-up research. In particular, ownership by the party with the larger valuation need not be optimal when the public good is partially excludable,[[65]](#cite_note-65) when both NGO and government may be indispensable,[[66]](#cite_note-66) or when the NGO and the government have different bargaining powers.[[67]](#cite_note-67) Moreover, the investment technology can matter for the optimal ownership structure when there are bargaining frictions,[[68]](#cite_note-68) when the parties interact repeatedly[[69]](#cite_note-69) or when the parties are asymmetrically informed.[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71)

## Influence on world affairs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-governmental_organization&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Influence on world affairs")]

[![Group of people (mostly women) in a room, with a woman speaking into a microphone](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/World_NGO_Day_Afghanistan.jpg/330px-World_NGO_Day_Afghanistan.jpg)](/wiki/File:World_NGO_Day_Afghanistan.jpg)

World NGO Day 2014 in [Afghanistan](/wiki/Islamic_Republic_of_Afghanistan "Islamic Republic of Afghanistan")

> Today we celebrate the World NGO Day, we celebrate the key civil society's contribution to public space and their unique ability to give voice to those who would have went [sic] otherwise unheard.

[European Commission Vice-President](/wiki/Vice-President_of_the_European_Commission "Vice-President of the European Commission") [Federica Mogherini](/wiki/Federica_Mogherini "Federica Mogherini"), commemorating the 2017 World NGO Day in [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels")[[72]](#cite_note-72)

Service-delivery NGOs provide public goods and services which governments of developing countries are unable to provide due to a lack of resources. They may be contractors or collaborate with government agencies to reduce the cost of public goods. Capacity-building NGOs affect "culture, structure, projects and daily operations".[[73]](#cite_note-73)

Advocacy and public-education NGOs aim to modify behavior and ideas through communication, crafting messages to promote social, political, or environmental changes (and as news organisations have cut foreign bureaux, many NGOs have begun to expand into news reporting).[[74]](#cite_note-74) Movement NGOs mobilize the public and coordinate large-scale collective activities to advance an activist agenda.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

Since the end of the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"), more NGOs in developed countries have pursued international outreach. By being involved in local and national social resistance, they have influenced domestic policy change in the developing world.[[76]](#cite_note-76) Specialized NGOs have forged partnerships, built networks, and found policy niches.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

### Track II diplomacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-governmental_organization&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Track II diplomacy")]

[Track II diplomacy](/wiki/Track_II_diplomacy "Track II diplomacy") (or dialogue) is transnational coordination by non-official members of the government, including [epistemic communities](/wiki/Epistemic_community "Epistemic community") and former policymakers or analysts. It aims to help policymakers and policy analysts reach a common solution through unofficial discussions. Unlike official diplomacy, conducted by government officials, diplomats, and elected leaders, Track II diplomacy involves experts, scientists, professors and other figures who are not part of government affairs.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### World NGO Day

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-governmental_organization&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: World NGO Day")]

World NGO Day, which is observed annually on 27 February, was recognised on 17 April 2010 by 12 countries of the IX [Baltic Sea](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea") NGO Forum at the eighth Summit of the Baltic Sea States in [Vilnius](/wiki/Vilnius "Vilnius"), Lithuania.[[78]](#cite_note-78) It was internationally recognised on 28 February 2014 in [Helsinki](/wiki/Helsinki "Helsinki"), Finland by [United Nations Development Programme](/wiki/United_Nations_Development_Programme "United Nations Development Programme") administrator and former [Prime Minister of New Zealand](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_New_Zealand "Prime Minister of New Zealand") [Helen Clark](/wiki/Helen_Clark "Helen Clark").[[79]](#cite_note-unric.org-79)[[80]](#cite_note-80)[[81]](#cite_note-81)

#### Diplomacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-governmental_organization&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Diplomacy")]

In the context of NGOs (Non-Governmental Organizations), diplomacy refers to the practice of building and maintaining partnerships with other organizations, stakeholders, and governments to achieve common objectives related to social or environmental issues.[[82]](#cite_note-82)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

NGOs often work in complex environments, where multiple stakeholders have different interests and goals. Diplomacy allows NGOs to navigate these complex environments and engage in constructive dialogue with different actors to promote understanding, build consensus, and facilitate cooperation.[[83]](#cite_note-83)[[84]](#cite_note-84)

Effective NGO diplomacy involves building trust, fostering dialogue, and promoting transparency and accountability. NGOs may engage in diplomacy through various means such as including advocacy, lobbying, partnerships, and negotiations. By working collaboratively with other organizations and stakeholders, NGOs can achieve greater impact and reach their goals more effectively.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-governmental_organization&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Criticism")]

### Negative outcomes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-governmental_organization&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Negative outcomes")]

[Tanzanian](/wiki/Tanzania "Tanzania") author and academic [Issa G. Shivji](/wiki/Issa_G._Shivji "Issa G. Shivji") has criticised NGOs in two essays: "Silences in NGO discourse: The role and future of NGOs in Africa" and "Reflections on NGOs in Tanzania: What we are, what we are not and what we ought to be". Shivji writes that despite the good intentions of NGO leaders and activists, he is critical of the "objective effects of actions, regardless of their intentions".[[85]](#cite_note-85) According to Shivji, the rise of NGOs is part of a [neoliberal](/wiki/Neoliberal "Neoliberal") paradigm and not motivated purely by altruism; NGOs want to change the world without understanding it, continuing an [imperial](/wiki/Imperialism "Imperialism") relationship.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In his study of NGO involvement in [Mozambique](/wiki/Mozambique "Mozambique"), James Pfeiffer addresses their negative effects on the country's health. According to Pfeiffer, NGOs in Mozambique have "fragmented the local health system, undermined local control of health programs, and contributed to growing local social inequality".[[86]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-86) They can be uncoordinated, creating parallel projects which divert health-service workers from their normal duties to instead serve the NGOs. This undermines local primary-healthcare efforts, and removes the government's ability to maintain agency over its health sector.[[86]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-86) Pfeiffer suggested a collaborative model of the NGO and the DPS (the Mozambique Provincial Health Directorate); the NGO should be "formally held to standard and adherence within the host country", reduce "showcase" projects and unsustainable parallel programs.[[86]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-86)

In her 1997 *[Foreign Affairs](/wiki/Foreign_Affairs "Foreign Affairs")* article, [Jessica Mathews](/wiki/Jessica_Mathews "Jessica Mathews") wrote: "For all their strengths, NGOs are special interests. The best of them ... often suffer from tunnel vision, judging every public act by how it affects their particular interest".[[87]](#cite_note-87) NGOs are unencumbered by policy trade-offs.[[88]](#cite_note-88)

According to [Vijay Prashad](/wiki/Vijay_Prashad "Vijay Prashad"), since the 1970s "the World Bank, under [Robert McNamara](/wiki/Robert_McNamara "Robert McNamara"), championed the NGO as an alternative to the state, leaving intact global and regional relations of power and production."[[89]](#cite_note-89)

They have been questioned as "too much of a good thing".[[90]](#cite_note-Werker-90) Eric Werker and Faisal Ahmed made three critiques of NGOs in developing nations. Too many NGOs in a nation (particularly one ruled by a warlord) reduces an NGO's influence, since it can easily be replaced by another NGO. Resource allocation and outsourcing to local organizations in international-development projects incurs expenses for an NGO, lessening the resources and money available to the intended beneficiaries. NGO missions tend to be paternalistic, as well as expensive.[[90]](#cite_note-Werker-90)

The [tax-exempt](/wiki/Tax-exempt "Tax-exempt") status of NGOs can result in the unintended consequence of negative value for society.[[91]](#cite_note-o561-91)

### Foreign influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-governmental_organization&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Foreign influence")]

See also: [Foreign funding of NGOs](/wiki/Foreign_funding_of_NGOs "Foreign funding of NGOs")

NGOs have been accused of preserving [imperialism](/wiki/Imperialism "Imperialism")[[92]](#cite_note-92) (sometimes operating in a [racialized](/wiki/Racialization "Racialization") manner in [Third World](/wiki/Third_World "Third World") countries), with a function similar to that of the clergy during the colonial era. Political philosopher [Peter Hallward](/wiki/Peter_Hallward "Peter Hallward") has called them an aristocratic form of politics,[[93]](#cite_note-93) noting that [ActionAid](/wiki/ActionAid "ActionAid") and [Christian Aid](/wiki/Christian_Aid "Christian Aid") "effectively condoned the [2004 US-backed] coup" against an elected government in Haiti and are the "humanitarian face of imperialism".[[94]](#cite_note-94) Movements in the [Global South](/wiki/Global_South "Global South") (such as [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa")'s [Western Cape Anti-Eviction Campaign](/wiki/Western_Cape_Anti-Eviction_Campaign "Western Cape Anti-Eviction Campaign")) have refused to work with NGOs, concerned that doing so would compromise their autonomy.[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-96) NGOs have been accused of weakening people by allowing their funders to prioritize stability over social justice.[[97]](#cite_note-97)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Finally_an_honest_days_labor_for_an_NGO_%28463962755%29.jpg/250px-Finally_an_honest_days_labor_for_an_NGO_%28463962755%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Finally_an_honest_days_labor_for_an_NGO_(463962755).jpg)

Workers from an NGO participating in local community work - some critics allege that NGOs prioritize their special interests over community wellbeing.

They have been accused of being designed by, and used as extensions of, the foreign-policy instruments of some Western countries and groups of countries.[[98]](#cite_note-98)[[99]](#cite_note-99) Russian president [Vladimir Putin](/wiki/Vladimir_Putin "Vladimir Putin") made that accusation at the 43rd [Munich Security Conference](/wiki/Munich_Security_Conference "Munich Security Conference") in 2007, saying that NGOs "are formally independent but they are purposefully financed and therefore under control".[[100]](#cite_note-100)
According to Michael Bond, "Most large NGOs, such as Oxfam, the Red Cross, Cafod and ActionAid, are striving to make their aid provision more sustainable. But some, mostly in the US, are still exporting the ideologies of their backers."[[101]](#cite_note-101)

NGOs have been challenged as not representing the needs of the developing world, diminishing the "Southern voice" and preserving the [North–South divide](/wiki/North%E2%80%93South_divide_in_the_World "North–South divide in the World").[[102]](#cite_note-linden-102) The equality of relationships between northern and southern parts of an NGO, and between southern and northern NGOs working in partnership, has been questioned; the north may lead in advocacy and resource mobilization, and the south delivers services in the developing world.[[102]](#cite_note-linden-102) The needs of the developing world may not be addressed appropriately, as northern NGOs do not consult (or participate in) partnerships or assign unrepresentative priorities.[[103]](#cite_note-103) NGOs have been accused of damaging the public sector in target countries, such as mismanagement resulting in the breakdown of public healthcare systems.[[86]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-86)

#### "Foreign agent" laws and GONGOs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-governmental_organization&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: \"Foreign agent\" laws and GONGOs")]

In the 21st century, a significant challenge for NGOs has been the rise of laws designed to restrict their activities, often under the pretext of protecting national sovereignty. A common tactic is the implementation of "foreign agent laws," which compel NGOs receiving any amount of international funding to register as "foreign agents." This term carries strong negative connotations, and registration often leads to prohibitive levels of government scrutiny, public distrust, and the eventual closure of the organization. Russia's 2012 foreign agent law is a prominent example, having been used to systematically dismantle civil society, including human rights groups and environmental advocates.[[104]](#cite_note-104) Similar laws have been implemented or proposed in other countries, including Hungary, Georgia, and Nicaragua, and are criticized by the UN and civil society groups as tools to suppress dissent.[[105]](#cite_note-105)

Conversely, some governments create what are known as [GONGOs](/wiki/Government-organized_non-governmental_organization "Government-organized non-governmental organization") (Government-Organized Non-Governmental Organizations). These are organizations created and sponsored by a state to appear as independent civil society. GONGOs are often used by authoritarian regimes to advocate for state policies at international forums like the United Nations, co-opting the language of human rights to provide a false sense of legitimacy and counter criticism from genuine, independent NGOs.[[106]](#cite_note-106)

### Spreading misinformation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-governmental_organization&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Spreading misinformation")]

NGOs have been accused of using [misinformation](/wiki/Misinformation "Misinformation") in their campaigns out of self-interest. According to Doug Parr of [Greenpeace](/wiki/Greenpeace "Greenpeace"), there had been "a tendency among our critics to say that science is the only decision-making tool ... but political and commercial interests are using science as a cover for getting their way."[[107]](#cite_note-bond323-107) Former policy-maker for the German branch of [Friends of the Earth](/wiki/Friends_of_the_Earth "Friends of the Earth") Jens Katjek said, "If NGOs want the best for the environment, they have to learn to compromise."[[107]](#cite_note-bond323-107)

### Challenges to legitimacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-governmental_organization&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Challenges to legitimacy")]

Legitimacy, an important asset of an NGO, is its perception as an "independent voice".[[108]](#cite_note-108)[[109]](#cite_note-2002aHD-109) Neera Chandhoke wrote in a *[Journal of World-Systems Research](/wiki/Journal_of_World-Systems_Research "Journal of World-Systems Research")* article, "To put the point starkly: are the citizens of countries of the South and their needs represented in global civil society, or are citizens as well as their needs constructed by practices of representation? And when we realize that INGOs hardly ever come face to face with the people whose interests and problems they represent, or that they are not accountable to the people they represent, matters become even more troublesome."[[110]](#cite_note-110)

An NGO's funding affects its legitimacy, and they have become increasingly dependent on a limited number of donors.[[111]](#cite_note-1996HD-111) Competition for funds has increased, in addition to the expectations of donors who may add conditions threatening an NGO's independence.[[112]](#cite_note-112) Dependence on official aid may dilute "the willingness of NGOs to speak out on issues which are unpopular with governments",[[109]](#cite_note-2002aHD-109) and changes in NGO funding sources have altered their function.[[109]](#cite_note-2002aHD-109)

The scale and variety of activities in which NGOs participate have grown rapidly since 1980, and particularly since 1990.[[113]](#cite_note-113) NGOs need to balance centralization and decentralization. Centralizing NGOs, particularly at the international level, can assign a common theme or set of goals. It may also be advantageous to decentralize an NGO, increasing its chances of responding flexibly and effectively to local issues by implementing projects which are modest in scale, easily monitored, produce immediate benefits, and where all involved know that corruption would be punished.[[114]](#cite_note-114)

[Embezzlement](/wiki/Embezzlement "Embezzlement"), [fraud](/wiki/Fraud "Fraud") and [financial mismanagement](/wiki/Financial_mismanagement "Financial mismanagement") reduce credibility of NGOs.[[115]](#cite_note-b987-115)

Referring to the NGO culture in Germany in 2025, with many NGO receiving government funding while at the same time pretending to be independent, Ben Krischke wrote in [Cicero](/wiki/Cicero_(magazine) "Cicero (magazine)"), the transcription as "Near-Governmental-Organisaton" would be more appropriate. He pointed out, that the concept developed over time under the [Angela Merkel](/wiki/Angela_Merkel "Angela Merkel") administration, with the parties in government being unable to use taxpayer money for partisan activities, directly or via subsidiaries, the number of such activities, for which Krischke gave the example of several "Reporting point"-projects for "incidents below the criminal threshold", somehow rose. A transparency initiative, trying to unravel the path of taxpayer money to various NGOs, is consequently facing substantial opposition by NGO activists from the left.[[116]](#cite_note-116)
